references.title: Motives for dropping out from higher education—An analysis of bachelor's degree students in Germany

extracted.Courses: 4 majors: Engineering, STEM, Law, Economics and Social Sciences, Linguistics and cultural Sciences
extracted.Data sources:
Student Interviews. Data is divided into main categories:

1. Interest/ Expectations

2. Performance/ Requirements

3. Financial Aspects

4. Study Conditions

5. Job Alternative/ Career

6. Personal/ Family Aspects
extracted.Theoretical Grounding: Vincent Tinto’s Model for Student Attrition
extracted.Features: [Drop Interest Field, Drop Expect, Drop Wish Practice, Drop No Practice, Drop Exam, Drop Require, Drop Material, drop Perform, Drop Activ, Drop Financial, Drop Moneymaking, Drop Anonym, Drop Overcrowd, Drop Orga, Drop Tuition, Drop Job, Drop Suitability, Drop Interest Job, Drop Opportunities, Drop Child, Drop Family, Drop Ill, Drop Pregnant, Drop Abroad]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The NEPS dataset conducts interviews with their students and the authors use this dataset. While there is some worry with pre-processing such as identification of different types of Missing Value, authors use PCA on the feature set to reduce dimensionality.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Silhouette Score: 0.634 - 8 Clusters wih reduced Feature Set}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Clusters 1 and 2: The largest clusters with a mix of all reasons for dropout. Difference is that Cluster 2 has a big prevalence of Family Reasons while Cluster 1 doesn’t.

Clusters 3 and 4: These students reported a combined of 3 or 4 motives for dropout. Cluster 3 specific is mostly comprised of second and third year droouts who argued for study-related reasons.

Cluster 5: Students who reported interest, expectations and performance rrelated problems - mainly early dropouts.

Cluster 6: Cited study conditions along wqith family/personal motives for dropping out.

Cluster 7: Mostly lack of interest and unfulfilled expectations.

Cluster 8: Early dropouts who leave because of mainly personal or family reasons.
extracted.Implications:
Programmes may focus on addressing the most relevant reasons for student dropout: 

1. The gap in expectation vs reality.

2. Take care of possibly unrealistic expectations on study requirements. 

Both could be achieved by greater comunication between university and Highschools.

Moreover, depending on program type, htere are differences in Dropout. Thus, the emasures that should be taken for distinct programs should probably be accounted for - STEM students foten leave for personal/financial reasons.
extracted.Comments:
Authors look to cluster different motives for Dropout. 

Authors do a series of discussions of motives for dropout according to different individual characteristics. Clustering is used afterward to cluster dropout motives using all predictors.
extracted.Limitations:
Panel attrition and missing values on motive for dropout.

Small sample size, that could benefit from more instances.

Exclusive use of Hierarchical Clsutering without attempting other approaches.

Subpar results on entire feature subset. Usage of PCA to bypass feature selection/Feature engineering.

annotations.text: Dropout is important to control for, so authors analyze the different reasons that may justify dropout from a higher education institution. The main reason authors found were interest and performance in an undergraduate program. Authors look at dropout using hierarchical clustering and identify multiple motives behind it.
